1.synthesis: 'The elder to the beloved Gaius, whom I love in truth' (v.1). The health-wish follows: 'Beloved, I pray that all may go well with you and that you may be in good health, just as it is going well with your soul' (v.2). The ground of joy: 'For I rejoiced greatly when the brothers came and testified to your truth, as indeed you are walking in the truth. I have no greater joy than to hear that my children are walking in the truth' (vv.3–4). Unlike 2 John, which addressed a community facing doctrinal danger, 3 John addresses an individual — Gaius — whose faithfulness in truth and hospitality has been reported back to the elder by traveling brothers. The letter is among the most personal in the NT, commending a specific individual, censuring another by name, and recommending a third.
1.historical_context: The health-wish of v.2 ('I pray that all may go well with you and that you may be in good health') is a standard Hellenistic letter formula; examples abound in Greek papyri from the period. Polycarp's letter to the Philippians opens similarly. The formula is not emptied of meaning by its conventionality: in the ancient Mediterranean world where illness was ubiquitous and life expectancy short, the prayer for health and good going was a genuine expression of care. The itinerant brothers who reported on Gaius (v.3) represent the early church's traveling teacher network — the primary information conduit between scattered house-church communities. Their oral testimony (martyrounton) about Gaius's truth-walking functions as a kind of independent confirmation that validates the elder's love for one he may not have visited recently. The 'my children' of v.4 is apostolic language: Paul uses the same phrase for his converts (1 Corinthians 4:14).
1.literary_structure: The four-verse greeting is unusually personal for an ancient letter: the threefold repetition of 'beloved' (vv.1, 2, 5, 11) throughout the letter signals the relational warmth that distinguishes it from the more guarded address of 2 John. The structure moves from address and love-declaration (v.1) through prayer for physical well-being proportionate to spiritual well-being (v.2) through the joy that grounds the prayer (vv.3–4). The parallel between physical health and spiritual health in v.2 is not merely conventional: the letter's concern throughout will be with the health of the community's hospitality practice and the disease of Diotrephes's self-promoting pride — the soul's health has community-structural consequences. The 'no greater joy' of v.4 is the letter's emotional key: it frames everything that follows as the elder's deep investment in the truth-walking of those he has fathered in the faith.
1.theological_application: Gill on the elder's identification: 'The elder is the writer of this epistle, the Apostle John, who so styles himself on account of his age, and office, as in the preceding epistle.' The title 'elder' in both letters is a pastoral self-identification rather than an assertion of apostolic rank — John writes as one who serves alongside those he addresses, even as his authority is evident in the letter's commendations and censures. The health-prayer of v.2 — 'just as it is going well with your soul' — makes the soul's welfare the standard against which physical welfare is measured. Matthew Henry reads this as a pastoral teaching: it would be a great mercy if the outward man prospered no more than the inward man prospers, for many who prosper outwardly are poor in soul; and it would be a great affliction if the inward man fared no better than the outward, for the soul's riches are far more durable. The elder's prayer for Gaius's physical health is informed by his knowledge that the soul is flourishing — and that is the ground of his confidence.
1.christology: The 'walking in the truth' that gives the elder 'no greater joy' is specifically walking in the truth that is Christ (John 14:6) — the same truth that the Johannine letters have consistently defended against Docetic reduction. Gaius's truth-walking is not a general ethical virtue but a specifically christological alignment: he holds the confession that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh (2 John 7), and his hospitality to the traveling brothers who proclaim that confession is the embodied expression of his christological fidelity. The elder's joy in hearing this is itself christologically grounded: the fullness of joy that 1 John 1:4 identifies as the telos of the apostolic proclamation is experienced precisely when those who have received the proclamation are found to be walking in the truth it declares.

5.synthesis: 'Beloved, it is a faithful thing you do in all your efforts for the brothers, especially for strangers. They have testified to your love before the church. You will do well to send them on their journey in a manner worthy of God, for they have gone out for the sake of the Name, accepting nothing from the Gentiles. Therefore we ought to support such people, so that we may be fellow workers for the truth' (vv.5–8). The four verses ground Gaius's hospitality practice in its theological significance: the traveling brothers go out 'for the Name,' which means supporting them is participating in the mission of the Name.
5.historical_context: The hospitality that Gaius extended to the traveling brothers was the ancient church's vital support system. The Greek ethical ideal of xenia (hospitality to strangers) and the Jewish mitzvah of hachnasat orchim (welcoming guests) both formed the cultural background for this practice, but early Christianity gave it distinctive theological motivation. The 'send them on their way' (propempsas) of v.6 is a technical term in early Christianity for the act of provisioning and providing travel assistance to missionaries departing for the next destination (Acts 15:3; Romans 15:24; 1 Corinthians 16:6). The brothers' acceptance of 'nothing from the Gentiles' (v.7) reflects an early Christian missionary policy designed to prevent charges of mercenary motives (1 Corinthians 9:12; 2 Corinthians 11:7).
5.literary_structure: The section is structured as a commendation-grounding-implication sequence: the commendation (faithful hospitality, v.5) is grounded in the brothers' own testimony (they reported Gaius's love to the church, v.6a) which then generates an exhortation (send them worthily, v.6b) grounded in the theological reason (they go for the Name, v.7) which generates the community obligation (we ought to support them, v.8). The final clause — 'so that we may be fellow workers for the truth' — is the theological capstone: Gaius's hospitality is not merely generosity but co-participation in the apostolic mission. The word 'co-workers' (synergoi) is a Pauline term for those who actively advance the gospel alongside the apostle (Philippians 4:3).
5.theological_application: Gill on v.5: 'Beloved, thou doest faithfully … in all his beneficence and charity he acted the upright part; he did not do it in an hypocritical way, to be seen of men, and gain applause from them, but from a principle of love, and with a view to the glory of God.' The faithfulness John commends is not merely the performance of hospitality but the inner orientation of it — done from love, not from the desire for credit. Barnes on 'in a manner worthy of God' (v.6): 'That is, as becomes those who are the servants of God … in a way that God would approve. This is the highest standard which can be applied to the conduct of Christians, in any respect, in their intercourse with others.' To send the brothers 'worthy of God' is to make the standard of hospitality not cultural convention (how hosts normally provision travelers) but the character of God himself — generous, glad, abundant.
5.christology: 'For they have gone out for the sake of the Name, accepting nothing from the Gentiles' (v.7) — 'the Name' is the divine Name of Jesus, the basis and content of the entire mission. The phrase appears in Acts 5:41 (rejoicing to suffer dishonor for the Name) and captures the christological concentration of early Christian mission: the missionaries do not travel for a cause or a doctrine but for a person whose Name is the only name given under heaven by which humanity can be saved (Acts 4:12). Supporting these missionaries is therefore not merely social charity but christological participation: 'co-workers for the truth' (v.8) means sharing in the work that the truth who is Christ is doing in the world through his sent-out servants. Gaius's hospitality is therefore a christological act, making him a participant in the mission of the Name.

9.synthesis: 'I have written something to the church, but Diotrephes, who likes to put himself first, does not acknowledge our authority. So if I come, I will bring up what he is doing, talking wicked nonsense against us. And not content with that, he refuses to welcome the brothers, and also stops those who want to welcome them and puts them out of the church' (vv.9–10). The response: 'Beloved, do not imitate evil but imitate good. Whoever does good is from God; whoever does evil has not seen God' (v.11). The commendation: 'Demetrius has received a good testimony from everyone, and from the truth itself. We also add our testimony, and you know that our testimony is true' (v.12).
9.historical_context: Diotrephes 'who likes to put himself first' (philoproteuo) represents the emergence of local congregational autonomy that resisted the authority of the itinerant apostolic elder. The conflict between the elder's authority (rooted in the apostolic tradition) and Diotrephes's local authority reflects a structural tension in early Christianity: as the apostolic generation aged and died, local leaders with no personal connection to the eyewitnesses began asserting independence. Diotrephes's offenses escalate through vv.9–10: (1) refusing to acknowledge the elder's authority; (2) spreading malicious talk against the elder; (3) refusing hospitality to the traveling brothers; (4) preventing others from welcoming them; (5) expelling those who do welcome them. The triple commendation of Demetrius in v.12 — by everyone, by the truth itself, by the elder's own testimony — is the literary and pastoral counterweight to Diotrephes's self-promotion.
9.literary_structure: The section is the letter's central conflict-and-resolution movement. Diotrephes is described (vv.9–10) and then answered not directly but through the call to imitate the good rather than the evil (v.11). The Demetrius commendation (v.12) provides the positive exemplar to replace the negative one: where Diotrephes sought preeminence and rejected testimony, Demetrius received good testimony from all sources. The triple witness structure of v.12 is juridically precise — everyone, the truth itself, and the elder — providing the three-witness confirmation required by Deuteronomy 19:15. The elder's conclusion ('you know that our testimony is true') is an appeal to Gaius's own experience: he has seen the brothers' conduct and can verify what the elder says.
9.theological_application: Gill on Diotrephes: 'Follow not, or do not imitate the particular evil or evils in Diotrephes; as his pride, ambition, love of preeminence, and tyrannical government in the church, and especially his hard heartedness, cruelty, and inhospitality to the poor saints.' The love of preeminence (philoproteuo) is identified by Gill as the root of all Diotrephes's specific offenses: the desire to be first is the spring from which rejection of authority, malicious talk, and inhospitality all flow. Gill on Demetrius: 'Demetrius hath good report of all men … he was of a quite different cast from Diotrephes, and therefore the apostle makes mention of him to Gaius, to be followed by him, and not the other.' The letter's pastoral strategy is to present Gaius with two figures to imitate or reject: Diotrephes (the way of evil, the way of the one who has not seen God) and Demetrius (the way of good, the way of the one who is from God).
9.christology: 'Whoever does good is from God; whoever does evil has not seen God' (v.11) — the two conditions trace back to their ultimate sources: origin from God (the new birth) and vision of God (the transforming encounter with the divine character). The statement echoes 1 John 3:6 and 3:10's two-children taxonomy: the children of God practice righteousness, the children of the devil do not. Diotrephes's pattern of self-promotion, rejection of the apostolic word, and inhospitality to the brothers who go out for the Name amounts to not having seen God — whose own character is the opposite: self-giving, receptive of the Son, hospitable to the point of 'sending them on their way in a manner worthy of God.' Demetrius, commended by the truth itself, represents the one who has been shaped by the truth who is Christ into the pattern of christological goodness that the letter has been commending throughout.

13.synthesis: 'I had much to write to you, but I would rather not write with pen and ink. I hope to see you soon, and we will speak face to face. Peace to you. The friends greet you. Greet the friends, each by name' (vv.13–14). The closing uses the same preference for face-to-face encounter over writing that 2 John 12 expresses, but the final phrase — 'greet the friends, each by name' — is unique in the NT, giving the close an unusually intimate and personal character: the elder knows the names of the individuals in Gaius's community and wants them personally greeted.
13.historical_context: The formula 'I hope to see you soon, and we will speak face to face' (stoma pros stoma, mouth to mouth) is the same as 2 John 12 and reflects the ancient world's valuation of personal presence over written communication. In the context of the letter's conflict with Diotrephes, the elder's announcement that he hopes to come in person (v.10: 'if I come, I will bring up what he is doing') gives the closing its pastoral urgency. The 'friends' (philoi) who send greetings and are to be greeted are a distinctive term — used elsewhere in the NT for Jesus's disciples (John 15:14–15: 'You are my friends … I have called you friends') — suggesting that the Johannine community used 'friends' as a self-designation alongside 'brothers' and 'beloved.' The 'each by name' (v.14) suggests that the elder has detailed knowledge of Gaius's community — names he knows, individuals he has met or knows by report.
13.literary_structure: The two-verse close is structurally parallel to 2 John 12–13: both prefer face-to-face to written communication, both close with greetings from a sister-community. But 3 John's close is distinctly more personal: 2 John closes with 'the children of your elect sister greet you,' while 3 John closes with 'the friends greet you; greet the friends, each by name.' The personalizing gesture of greeting 'each by name' is the letter's final act of intimacy — the entire letter has been addressed to Gaius as a named individual, and it closes by extending that personal recognition to the unnamed individuals around him. The peace-wish 'peace to you' stands between the face-to-face hope and the greetings, a brief christological benediction that carries the weight of the entire letter's concerns about truth, hospitality, and the health of the community.
13.theological_application: Gill on the preference for personal communication: 'I have many things to write, with regard to churches, and particular persons, and concerning hospitality to the poor brethren: not that he had any new doctrine, or any other than what they had heard from the beginning, to communicate to them by word of mouth, for this he denies.' The letter's restriction is not a withholding of new revelation but a wise pastoral judgment: some things are better said in person than committed to writing. Barnes on the close: 'This epistle closes, as the second does, with a statement that he had many things to say, but that he preferred waiting till he should see him rather than put them on paper. Perhaps there were some things which he wished to say which he would not like to have exposed in a written letter.' The elder's care for the community's internal affairs — including the Diotrephes situation — is evident: some pastoral interventions require the presence of the pastor, not the distance of the letter.
13.christology: 'Peace to you' — the closing benediction of 3 John is the same peace-wish that closes 2 John, 1 John (implicit throughout), and the Gospel's resurrection appearances: 'Peace be with you' (John 20:19, 21, 26). The peace the risen Christ breathed upon his disciples in the upper room is the same peace the elder invokes for Gaius. The 'friends' whom the elder greets and asks to be greeted in turn by name are the 'friends' whom Jesus named when he said 'I have called you friends' (John 15:15) — the community of those who know what the Son has received from the Father and has made known to them. The letter's final word is thus the most personal in the NT's closing formulas: not the universal 'all the saints' or 'the brothers' but each by name — each one known to the elder, each one named by the God who calls his own sheep by name (John 10:3).
